does: This paper introduces the first comprehensive framework for evaluating source attribution in LLM-generated research reports. The authors develop an AST parser that extracts inline citations from Markdown-formatted outputs at scale, then systematically evaluates citations across three dimensions: Link Works (URL accessibility), Relevant Content (topical alignment between claim and source), and Fact Check (factual accuracy against retrieved source material). They benchmark 14 LLMs—both closed and open-source—using LLM-as-a-judge evaluation with rubric-based scoring, moving beyond isolated claim verification to a closed-loop approach that retrieves actual cited content and compares it against the generated assertions. The framework enables reproducible, large-scale assessment of citation reliability across diverse models and research synthesis tasks.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining hallucinations and factuality in LLMs, particularly in knowledge-intensive tasks like information synthesis and report generation. It extends retrieval-augmented generation research by addressing the validation gap—prior RAG systems retrieved documents but did not verify whether retrieved content actually supported claimed citations or remained accessible. The paper responds to limitations in both self-citation approaches, which suffer from bias and lack verification, and earlier attribution evaluation methods that check individual claims without grounding them in cited sources. It connects to broader efforts in LLM evaluation and interpretability that seek to understand and measure when models faithfully represent external information versus fabricating or misrepresenting sources.
enables: This framework opens pathways for improving citation reliability in AI-assisted research tools and knowledge synthesis systems. By establishing measurable evaluation dimensions, it enables researchers to identify which models and architectures produce more verifiable citations, supporting the development of better deep research agents. The reproducible AST-based extraction method can be integrated into real-time citation verification systems and deployed as a filtering mechanism during generation. The benchmarking results create baselines for tracking progress in source attribution fidelity across model families and provide concrete targets for fine-tuning or retrieval augmentation improvements. Future work could leverage these evaluation insights to build better citation-aware training objectives, develop models explicitly trained to maximize citation quality, or create user-facing tools that flag potentially unreliable citations in research reports before publication.
